title: NOC 31201 – Chiropractors | GTR Immigration
description: Chiropractors are licensed healthcare professionals specializing in the diagnosis and treatment of neuromuscular and musculoskeletal disorders. Across Canada, demand for chiropractic care continues to rise due to increasing awareness of non-surgical pain management, sports rehabilitation, and preventive spine health. Classified under NOC 31201 (TEER 1), Chiropractors are eligible under:
duties: Conduct patient interviews and take medical histories
Perform postural, neurological, and orthopedic assessments
Order or interpret X-rays and imaging (where permitted)
Diagnose spinal, joint, and musculoskeletal disorders
Provide soft tissue therapy and complementary treatments (electrotherapy, acupuncture, massage, heat therapy)
Counsel patients on corrective exercises, posture, nutrition, and lifestyle modifications
Refer patients to other healthcare professionals when necessary
employment_requirements: To practice as a Chiropractor in Canada:
Minimum three years of undergraduate university study (or Quebec college diploma in natural sciences)
Completion of a four- or five-year Doctor of Chiropractic program from an accredited institution
Successful completion of national examinations administered by the Canadian Chiropractic Examining Board
Licensing by a provincial regulatory body (mandatory in all provinces and in Yukon)
Without provincial licensure, independent practice is not permitted.
job_outlook: This indicates a relatively balanced but stable employment outlook, with continued growth driven by:
Increased preference for drug-free pain treatment

pathways: Growing demand for non-invasive musculoskeletal care
Collaborative practice with physiotherapists, massage therapists, and medical doctors
Access to Canada’s stable healthcare system
additional_information: Private clinicsMultidisciplinary health centersSports medicine facilitiesRehabilitation clinicsWellness and physiotherapy practices
Typical annual earnings: $27,500–$122,400 CAD, depending on location, client base, specialization, and whether operating independently.
Urban centers offer strong private practice potential, while suburban and growing communities often present less competition.
